title: A Difficulty in the Concept of Social Welfare
review:
Arrow's impossibility theorem is one of the most elegant results in social choice theory. The paper demonstrates that no ranked voting system can satisfy a set of seemingly reasonable fairness criteria simultaneously.

Formally, let $N$ be a set of voters and $A$ a set of alternatives with $|A| \geq 3$. Arrow proves there is no social welfare function $f$ satisfying:

1. **Unrestricted domain**: $f$ is defined for all possible preference profiles.
2. **Pareto efficiency**: If every voter prefers $x$ to $y$, then the social ranking ranks $x$ above $y$.
3. **Independence of irrelevant alternatives**: The social preference between $x$ and $y$ depends only on individual preferences between $x$ and $y$.
4. **Non-dictatorship**: There is no single voter $i$ such that $i$'s preference always determines the social outcome.

The proof is remarkably concise for such a sweeping result. Arrow constructs a *decisive* set argument, showing that the conjunction of conditions (1)–(3) forces the decisive set to shrink to a single individual, violating (4).

$$\nexists\, f: \mathcal{L}(A)^N \to \mathcal{L}(A) \text{ satisfying (1)–(4)}$$

A foundational paper that reshaped welfare economics and political philosophy in one stroke.

title: The Problem of Social Cost
review:
Coase challenges the Pigouvian tradition head-on by arguing that externalities are reciprocal in nature. The so-called Coase Theorem — a label Coase never used himself — states that if transaction costs are zero and property rights are well defined, bargaining will lead to an efficient allocation regardless of the initial assignment of rights.

The paper is best read not as a policy prescription but as a thought experiment that highlights the critical role of transaction costs. When we observe real-world inefficiencies from externalities, the culprit is not the absence of a tax but the presence of transaction costs that prevent Coasean bargaining.

Coase's numerical examples, particularly the farmer-rancher problem, remain a staple of introductory law and economics courses. The depth of insight packed into what appears to be a straightforward argument is remarkable.

title: Equilibrium Points in N-Person Games
review:
A one-page masterpiece. Nash proves that every finite game has at least one equilibrium point in mixed strategies using Kakutani's fixed-point theorem.

Let $\Gamma = (N, (S_i)_{i \in N}, (u_i)_{i \in N})$ be a finite normal-form game. A **Nash equilibrium** is a strategy profile $\sigma^* = (\sigma_1^*, \ldots, \sigma_n^*)$ such that for every player $i$:

$$u_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}^*) \quad \forall \sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)$$

The proof maps the space of mixed strategy profiles into itself via a best-response correspondence and applies Kakutani's theorem to guarantee a fixed point. The brevity and elegance are astonishing — the entire argument fits on a single journal page.

This paper is the foundation stone of non-cooperative game theory.

title: Justice as Fairness
review:
This article previews the core ideas later developed in *A Theory of Justice*. Rawls introduces the original position and the veil of ignorance as devices for deriving principles of justice that rational agents would agree to under conditions of fairness.

The two principles Rawls arrives at are:

1. Each person has an equal right to the most extensive basic liberty compatible with a similar liberty for others.
2. Social and economic inequalities are to be arranged so that they are both (a) to the greatest benefit of the least advantaged and (b) attached to positions open to all under conditions of fair equality of opportunity.

The article is a cleaner, more concise statement than the later book. It's the ideal entry point for anyone approaching Rawlsian political philosophy for the first time.

title: Theory of Value: An Axiomatic Analysis of Economic Equilibrium
review:
Debreu's monograph provides the definitive axiomatic treatment of general equilibrium theory. The mathematical rigor is uncompromising — every assumption is stated explicitly, every theorem proved carefully.

The commodity space is modeled as $\mathbb{R}^l$ where $l$ is the number of commodities. Consumer preferences are represented by complete, transitive, continuous, and convex preorders on consumption sets $X_i \subset \mathbb{R}^l$.

The existence proof for competitive equilibrium uses Kakutani's fixed-point theorem applied to the excess demand correspondence. The welfare theorems follow as elegant corollaries.

While terse and demanding, this is essential reading for anyone serious about mathematical economics. Debreu strips away all narrative to reveal the logical skeleton of price theory.

title: On Formally Undecidable Propositions of Principia Mathematica and Related Systems
review:
Gödel's first incompleteness theorem asserts that any consistent formal system $F$ capable of expressing elementary arithmetic contains statements that are true but unprovable within $F$.

The key construction is the Gödel sentence $G_F$ which, via an ingenious self-referential encoding (Gödel numbering), effectively asserts: "I am not provable in $F$." If $F$ is consistent, then $G_F$ is true but not provable; if $F$ proves $G_F$, then $F$ is inconsistent.

$$F \vdash G_F \implies F \text{ is inconsistent}$$
$$F \nvdash G_F \implies G_F \text{ is true}$$

The second incompleteness theorem further shows that $F$ cannot prove its own consistency, i.e., $F \nvdash \text{Con}(F)$.

This paper shattered Hilbert's program and redrew the boundaries of mathematical knowledge. One of the most important intellectual achievements of the 20th century.

title: The Possibility of Social Choice
review:
Sen's Nobel lecture provides a masterful survey of social choice theory from Arrow's impossibility result through the capability approach. He argues that Arrow's result, while mathematically devastating, should not lead us to nihilism about social evaluation.

By relaxing the informational constraints — allowing interpersonal comparisons of utility, partial comparability, or functionings-based evaluation — we recover the possibility of reasoned social judgment.

Sen weaves together technical results and philosophical argument with characteristic grace. Essential reading as a map of the field.

title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
review:
Kant's *Groundwork* lays out the foundations of the categorical imperative in three formulations:

1. **Universal Law**: Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.
2. **Humanity**: Act so that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in that of another, always as an end and never merely as a means.
3. **Kingdom of Ends**: Act according to maxims of a universally legislating member of a merely possible kingdom of ends.

The progression from common moral knowledge through philosophical analysis to the metaphysics of morals is brilliantly structured. The concept of the *good will* as the only thing good without qualification remains one of the most striking opening claims in philosophy.

Dense, demanding, and inexhaustible.
